Name: rate_distortion_quantization
Title: Optimal Quantization Strategies for Model Compression via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing model weight quantization as a lossy source coding problem, we can derive optimal quantization strategies that minimize distortion while preserving task-relevant information, leading to better compression performance than existing heuristic methods.
Related Work: While existing work focuses on mixed-precision quantization and empirical heuristics, our proposal distinctly employs rate-distortion theory to derive theoretical limits on bits-per-weight and optimal quantization strategies. This theoretical foundation has not been systematically applied to the quantization of neural network weights in the literature.
Abstract: In the quest for efficient model compression, quantization has emerged as a pivotal technique to reduce the size of neural networks while maintaining performance. This paper proposes a novel framework that employs rate-distortion theory to derive optimal quantization strategies for model weights. By treating weight quantization as a lossy source coding problem, we analyze the trade-offs between the number of bits allocated per weight and the resulting distortion in task performance. We establish theoretical bounds on the minimal bits-per-weight required to maintain predictive sufficiency, focusing on task-relevant distortion measures. Our approach extends the existing empirical methods by providing a principled, information-theoretic basis for quantization, leading to more efficient compression techniques. We validate our framework through a series of experiments that compare the derived quantization strategies against state-of-the-art benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance in preserving accuracy while achieving significant model size reduction.
Experiments: 1. Derive theoretical bounds on bits-per-weight required for various neural network architectures and tasks, focusing on quantization error metrics.
2. Implement quantization strategies based on the derived bounds and compare them against standard mixed-precision quantization methods using a set of benchmark datasets.
3. Evaluate the performance of the proposed quantization strategies on downstream tasks (e.g., image classification, NLP) using metrics such as accuracy and F1 score.
4. Analyze the relationship between allocated bits and task-relevant distortion to validate the theoretical predictions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the assumptions made about the underlying distributions of weights and tasks, which could limit the generalizability of the findings. Additionally, the computational complexity of deriving optimal quantization strategies may pose challenges in practice, necessitating careful implementation.
